system_prompt:
You are Sunstein, a precise and efficient AI assistant.

Rules:
- Be concise. Skip explanations unless asked.
- Minimize token usage in every response.
- Use execute_code for batch operations, not one-at-a-time.
- Always use the cheapest model that can handle the task.
- Compress context regularly with /compress.
- Never repeat information already in the conversation.
- One-word answers when possible.
- No preamble, no postamble.

If the user asks about configuring, setting up, or using Hermes Agent itself, load the `hermes-agent` skill with skill_view(name='hermes-agent') before answering. Docs: https://hermes-agent.nousresearch.com/docs

You have persistent memory across sessions. Save durable facts using the memory tool: user preferences, environment details, tool quirks, and stable conventions. Memory is injected into every turn, so keep it compact and focused on facts that will still matter later.
Prioritize what reduces future user steering — the most valuable memory is one that prevents the user from having to correct or remind you again. User preferences and recurring corrections matter more than procedural task details.
Do NOT save task progress, session outcomes, completed-work logs, or temporary TODO state to memory; use session_search to recall those from past transcripts. If you've discovered a new way to do something, solved a problem that could be necessary later, save it as a skill with the skill tool.
Write memories as declarative facts, not instructions to yourself. 'User prefers concise responses' ✓ — 'Always respond concisely' ✗. 'Project uses pytest with xdist' ✓ — 'Run tests with pytest -n 4' ✗. Imperative phrasing gets re-read as a directive in later sessions and can cause repeated work or override the user's current request. Procedures and workflows belong in skills, not memory. After completing a complex task (5+ tool calls), fixing a tricky error, or discovering a non-trivial workflow, save the approach as a skill with skill_manage so you can reuse it next time.
When using a skill and finding it outdated, incomplete, or wrong, patch it immediately with skill_manage(action='patch') — don't wait to be asked. Skills that aren't maintained become liabilities.

## Skills (mandatory)
Before replying, scan the skills below. If a skill matches or is even partially relevant to your task, you MUST load it with skill_view(name) and follow its instructions. Err on the side of loading — it is always better to have context you don't need than to miss critical steps, pitfalls, or established workflows. Skills contain specialized knowledge — API endpoints, tool-specific commands, and proven workflows that outperform general-purpose approaches. Load the skill even if you think you could handle the task with basic tools like web_search or terminal. Skills also encode the user's preferred approach, conventions, and quality standards for tasks like code review, planning, and testing — load them even for tasks you already know how to do, because the skill defines how it should be done here.
Whenever the user asks you to configure, set up, install, enable, disable, modify, or troubleshoot Hermes Agent itself — its CLI, config, models, providers, tools, skills, voice, gateway, plugins, or any feature — load the `hermes-agent` skill first. It has the actual commands (e.g. `hermes config set …`, `hermes tools`, `hermes setup`) so you don't have to guess or invent workarounds.
If a skill has issues, fix it with skill_manage(action='patch').
After difficult/iterative tasks, offer to save as a skill. If a skill you loaded was missing steps, had wrong commands, or needed pitfalls you discovered, update it before finishing.

You are a CLI AI Agent. Try not to use markdown but simple text renderable inside a terminal. File delivery: there is no attachment channel — the user reads your response directly in their terminal. Do NOT emit MEDIA:/path tags (those are only intercepted on messaging platforms like Telegram, Discord, Slack, etc.; on the CLI they render as literal text). When referring to a file you created or changed, just state its absolute path in plain text; the user can open it from there.

function.name: memory
function.description:
Save durable information to persistent memory that survives across sessions. Memory is injected into future turns, so keep it compact and focused on facts that will still matter later.

WHEN TO SAVE (do this proactively, don't wait to be asked):
- User corrects you or says 'remember this' / 'don't do that again'
- User shares a preference, habit, or personal detail (name, role, timezone, coding style)
- You discover something about the environment (OS, installed tools, project structure)
- You learn a convention, API quirk, or workflow specific to this user's setup
- You identify a stable fact that will be useful again in future sessions

PRIORITY: User preferences and corrections > environment facts > procedural knowledge. The most valuable memory prevents the user from having to repeat themselves.

Do NOT save task progress, session outcomes, completed-work logs, or temporary TODO state to memory; use session_search to recall those from past transcripts.
If you've discovered a new way to do something, solved a problem that could be necessary later, save it as a skill with the skill tool.

TWO TARGETS:
- 'user': who the user is -- name, role, preferences, communication style, pet peeves
- 'memory': your notes -- environment facts, project conventions, tool quirks, lessons learned

ACTIONS: add (new entry), replace (update existing -- old_text identifies it), remove (delete -- old_text identifies it).

SKIP: trivial/obvious info, things easily re-discovered, raw data dumps, and temporary task state.

target.description: Which memory store: 'memory' for personal notes, 'user' for user profile.

content.description: The entry content. Required for 'add' and 'replace'.

old_text.description: Short unique substring identifying the entry to replace or remove.

function.name: skill_manage
function.description:
Manage skills (create, update, delete). Skills are your procedural memory — reusable approaches for recurring task types. New skills go to ~/.hermes/skills/; existing skills can be modified wherever they live.

Actions: create (full SKILL.md + optional category), patch (old_string/new_string — preferred for fixes), edit (full SKILL.md rewrite — major overhauls only), delete, write_file, remove_file.

Create when: complex task succeeded (5+ calls), errors overcome, user-corrected approach worked, non-trivial workflow discovered, or user asks you to remember a procedure.
Update when: instructions stale/wrong, OS-specific failures, missing steps or pitfalls found during use. If you used a skill and hit issues not covered by it, patch it immediately.

After difficult/iterative tasks, offer to save as a skill. Skip for simple one-offs. Confirm with user before creating/deleting.

Good skills: trigger conditions, numbered steps with exact commands, pitfalls section, verification steps. Use skill_view() to see format examples.

Pinned skills are off-limits — all write actions refuse with a message pointing the user to `hermes curator unpin <name>`. Don't try to route around this by renaming or recreating.

name.description: Skill name (lowercase, hyphens/underscores, max 64 chars). Must match an existing skill for patch/edit/delete/write_file/remove_file.

content.description: Full SKILL.md content (YAML frontmatter + markdown body). Required for 'create' and 'edit'. For 'edit', read the skill first with skill_view() and provide the complete updated text.

old_string.description: Text to find in the file (required for 'patch'). Must be unique unless replace_all=true. Include enough surrounding context to ensure uniqueness.

new_string.description: Replacement text (required for 'patch'). Can be empty string to delete the matched text.

replace_all.description: For 'patch': replace all occurrences instead of requiring a unique match (default: false).

category.description: Optional category/domain for organizing the skill (e.g., 'devops', 'data-science', 'mlops'). Creates a subdirectory grouping. Only used with 'create'.

file_path.description: Path to a supporting file within the skill directory. For 'write_file'/'remove_file': required, must be under references/, templates/, scripts/, or assets/. For 'patch': optional, defaults to SKILL.md if omitted.

file_content.description: Content for the file. Required for 'write_file'.

function.name: skill_view
function.description: Skills allow for loading information about specific tasks and workflows, as well as scripts and templates. Load a skill's full content or access its linked files (references, templates, scripts). First call returns SKILL.md content plus a 'linked_files' dict showing available references/templates/scripts. To access those, call again with file_path parameter.

name.description: The skill name (use skills_list to see available skills). For plugin-provided skills, use the qualified form 'plugin:skill' (e.g. 'superpowers:writing-plans').

file_path.description: OPTIONAL: Path to a linked file within the skill (e.g., 'references/api.md', 'templates/config.yaml', 'scripts/validate.py'). Omit to get the main SKILL.md content.

function.name: skills_list
function.description: List available skills (name + description). Use skill_view(name) to load full content.

category.description: Optional category filter to narrow results

# Dayah LitWorks — AI Agent Guide

## Quick Links
- **Live:** https://dayah.paragu-ai.com
- **Repo:** github.com/Ai-Whisperers/dayah-litworks
- **Docker service:** dayah-litworks_web (2 replicas)
- **VPS:** 72.61.44.159
- **Traefik:** Host(`dayah.paragu-ai.com`)

## Architecture
Cloudflare (DNS, SSL) → VPS → Traefik → dayah-litworks_web:3000

## Pages
13 pages: Home, Servicios, Premades, Portafolio, Blog (5 posts), Nosotros, FAQ, Contacto, Privacidad, Términos

## Design System
Dark theme (navy/plum background, #0a0a14/var(--background)). Primary: #94abd6 (muted blue). Secondary: #d43d5e (rose). Fonts: Playfair Display (headings), Inter (body). Voice: warm, creative, Spanish.

## Content
Content lives in `content/es.json` (and other locales when applicable).
All text, services, products, FAQ items are editable there.

## Build & Deploy
```bash
npm run build
docker build -t dayah-litworks:prod .
docker stack deploy -c docker-compose.yml dayah-litworks
```

## Critical Patterns
- All content in content/es.json and content/en.json
- No database — fully static
- WhatsApp is the primary CTA
- Dark theme with gradient accents

## Client Onboarding
See `docs/client-questionnaire.md` for the full onboarding questionnaire.
See `docs/brand-guide.md` for brand identity details.

{"code": "from hermes_tools import terminal, search_files

# Get all cover filenames (main books)
r = terminal("cd /tmp/dayah-litworks && ls public/dayah/cover*.*", timeout=10)
print("=== MAIN COVERS ===")
print(r["output"])

# Get all covers dir files
r2 = terminal("cd /tmp/dayah-litworks && ls public/dayah/covers/", timeout=10)
print("\n=== COVERS DIR (first 50) ===")
lines = r2["output"].strip().split('\n')
for l in lines[:50]:
    print(l)
print(f"... and {len(lines)-50} more")

# Check content/es.json for book data
r3 = search_files("public/dayah/covers/", path="/tmp/dayah-litworks", pattern="jpg|webp|png", target="files")
print(f"\n=== TOTAL COVERS FILES: {len(r3['matches'])} ===")

# Read es.json for currently published books
r4 = terminal("cd /tmp/dayah-litworks && python3 -c \"import json; d=json.load(open('content/es.json')); print(json.dumps({k: d[k] for k in list(d.keys())[:8]}, indent=2, ensure_ascii=False)[:3000])\"", timeout=10)
print("\n=== ES.JSON TOP KEYS ===")
print(r4["output"])
"}

print("=== PREMADES ===")

# Check blog entries for books mentioned
r2 = terminal("cd /tmp/dayah-litworks && python3 -c \"import json; d=json.load(open('content/es.json')); print(json.dumps(d.get('blog', 'NOT FOUND'), indent=2, ensure_ascii=False))\"", timeout=10)
print("\n=== BLOG ===")

# Check services section
r3 = terminal("cd /tmp/dayah-litworks && python3 -c \"import json; d=json.load(open('content/es.json')); s=d.get('home',{}).get('services',{}).get('items',[]); print(json.dumps(s, indent=2, ensure_ascii=False))\"", timeout=10)
print("\n=== SERVICES ===")
print(r3["output"][:3000])
"}

The site has **8 named books** in the portfolio but **239 cover images** in the `covers/` folder with Instagram-style filenames — these are likely the 400+ portadas Dayah has designed but they're not cataloged/named. {"code": "from hermes_tools import terminal

# Check the covers filenames more carefully — they might have FB/IG IDs we can match
r = terminal("cd /tmp/dayah-litworks && ls -la public/dayah/covers/ | head -10", timeout=10)
print(r["output"])

# Deduplicate by checking for .jpg + .webp pairs
r2 = terminal("cd /tmp/dayah-litworks && ls public/dayah/covers/*.jpg | wc -l && ls public/dayah/covers/*.webp | wc -l", timeout=10)
print(r2["output"])

# Check for any book-related data files, CSV, or docs
r3 = terminal("cd /tmp/dayah-litworks && find . -type f \( -name '*.csv' -o -name '*.xlsx' -o -name '*.tsv' -o -name '*.json' -o -name '*.md' \) ! -path './node_modules/*' ! -path './.git/*' | sort", timeout=10)
print("=== DATA FILES ===")
print(r3["output"])
"}

function.name: browser_console
function.arguments: {"expression": "Array.from(document.querySelectorAll('div[data-component-type=\"s-search-result\"]')).map(el => { const titleEl = el.querySelector('h2 a'); return { asin: el.dataset.asin, title: titleEl?.textContent?.trim(), url: titleEl?.href, price: el.querySelector('.a-price .a-offscreen')?.textContent }; })"}

> Complete inventory of books in the portfolio, with Amazon links and metadata.
> Generated: [REDACTED_PHONE]

---

## CONFIRMED BOOKS ON AMAZON

### Dayah Araujo (own books)

| # | Title | Author | Genre | ASIN | Amazon Link |
|---|-------|--------|-------|------|-------------|
| 1 | **Intense** (2nd Edition) | Dayah Araujo | Romance Erótico | B0957V2YVQ | https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0957V2YVQ |
| 2 | **Extrême** (Intense nº 2) | Dayah Araujo | Romance Erótico | B0D5C8CL3Y | https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0D5C8CL3Y |
| 3 | **Seduciendo al duque de Wellington** | Dayah Araujo | Romance Histórico | B09RGFRHD8 | https://www.amazon.com/dp/B09RGFRHD8 |
| 4 | **Al calor de un beso: Matrimonios forzados II** | Alma Lawson (cover by Dayah) | Romance | B0CNSGJT2J | https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0CNSGJT2J |

### Client Books (cover design by Dayah)
**NOTE:** Only Dayah's own books and "Al calor de un beso" were found on Amazon US. Client books listed below were NOT found on Amazon US during search.

| # | Title (site) | Author (site) | Genre | Amazon Status |
|---|--------------|---------------|-------|-------------|
| 5 | **Cómo Volver Loco a mi Guardaespaldas** | Meyling Soza | Romance Comedia | ❌ Not found on Amazon US. Similar title found by "Paula Da Rocha" — may have been renamed/re-published |
| 6 | **El Secreto que nos Unió** | Verónica Mengual | Romance | ❌ Not found on Amazon US. Verónica Mengual writes historical romance (Tierras Altas, Las Normas de la Regencia series) — this might be a different title/pseudonym |
| 7 | **Perdóname Padre o Hazme Tuya** | María González | Romance Oscuro | ❌ Not found on Amazon US |
| 8 | **Macarena y sus Ex** | Lucy Landa | Romance Comedia | ❌ Not found on Amazon US |
| 9 | **PassionFruit — Vol. I** | Ana Lucía Ramírez | Romance | ❌ Not found on Amazon US |
| 10 | **PassionFruit — Vol. II** | Ana Lucía Ramírez | Romance | ❌ Not found on Amazon US |

### Books by Dayah NOT in site portfolio (discovered on Amazon)

| # | Title | ASIN | Notes |
|---|-------|------|-------|
| 11 | **Seduciendo al duque de Wellington** | B09RGFRHD8 | Historical romance, NOT listed on the current website |
| 12 | **Al calor de un beso** (Matrimonios forzados II) | B0CNSGJT2J | By Alma Lawson, cover by Dayah. NOT on the site's portfolio |

---

## AUTHOR INFO

### Dayah Araujo Author Page
- **Amazon Author Central:** https://www.amazon.com/author/dayaharaujo
- **Amazon Store:** https://www.amazon.com/stores/Dayah-Araujo/author/B095BQSQRK
- **Amazon Author ID:** B095BQSQRK

### Verónica Mengual (client)
- Amazon author with 16+ books in historical romance
- Series: Tierras Altas, Las Normas de la Regencia, Sombras y Luces
- Covers designed by Dayah include: "El Secreto que nos Unió" (not found on Amazon — may be unpublished or listed under different title)
- Author link: https://www.amazon.com/stores/Veronica-Mengual/e/B0B4N8WJ97

---

## IMAGE INVENTORY (324 total)

### Main Named Covers (8 books × 2 formats = 16 files)
Stored in `public/dayah/`:

| Book | Image | Format | Used For |
|------|-------|--------|----------|
| Extrême | cover-extreme.png / .webp | PNG+WebP | Portfolio, Premade "Alas de Cristal" |
| Intense 2° Edición | cover-intense.png / .webp | PNG+WebP | Portfolio, Premade "Corazón de Cenizas" & "Galaxia Interior" |
| Cómo Volver Loco a mi Guardaespaldas | cover-volver-loco.jpg / .webp | JPG+WebP | Portfolio, Premade "El Último Código" |
| El Secreto que nos Unió | cover-secreto.png / .webp | PNG+WebP | Portfolio, Premade "Susurros del Bosque" |
| Perdóname Padre o Hazme Tuya | cover-perdoname.png / .webp | PNG+WebP | Portfolio |
| Macarena y sus Ex | cover-macarena.png / .webp | PNG+WebP | Portfolio, Premade "Sombras en el Espejo" |
| PassionFruit Vol. I | cover-pf-ebook-vol1.jpg / .webp | JPG+WebP | Portfolio |
| PassionFruit Vol. II | cover-pf-ebook-vol2.jpg / .webp | JPG+WebP | Portfolio |

### Unnamed Covers (239 images in `covers/`)
Instagram-sourced filenames (FB/IG media IDs). **None have Book/Use names assigned.** These are likely from:
- Dayah's own book promos (Intense, Extrême, Seduciendo al duque)
- Client books (the 6 listed above plus possibly others not on the portfolio)
- Social posts featuring books in mockups
- Premade designs for sale

### Social Images (58)
Promotional posts, book reveals, author announcements, testimonials.

### Mockups (3)
3D renderings of books for promotional use.

---

## ISSUES FOUND

### Critical
1. **239 covers in `covers/` are unnamed** — the IMAGES-INDEX.md has empty Book/Use and Slug columns for all. Someone needs to match each image to its book.
2. **Premade page reuses portfolio covers** — all 6 premades use the same images as portfolio items. Premades should have their own unique images (not real published books).
3. **`cover-intense.webp` is used for 2 different premades** — "Corazón de Cenizas" AND "Galaxia Interior" both point to the same image.

### Moderate
4. **Amazon links all point to author page** — every portfolio item's `amazonUrl` goes to generic `https://www.amazon.com/author/dayaharaujo` instead of specific book pages.
5. **Missing Dayah books on site** — "Seduciendo al duque de Wellington" and "Al calor de un beso" exist on Amazon but aren't in the portfolio.
6. **Duplicate formats** — most books have both original (PNG/JPG) + WebP. Total unique books ≈ 8 main + ~239 in covers dir = ~247 unique covers total.

### Minor
7. **cover-secreto.png is 3.3MB** — oversized, should be optimized.
8. **No ALT text or metadata** for any image.

---

## RECOMMENDATIONS

1. **Ask Dayah** to match the 239 cover images to book titles (one-time manual pass).
2. **Get real Amazon links** from the authors for each client book.
3. **Create unique premade images** — don't reuse real book covers as premade samples.
4. **Add missing Dayah books** ("Seduciendo al duque de Wellington") to the portfolio.
5. **Fix Amazon URLs** — point each book to its specific product page.
6. **Optimize large images** — `cover-secreto.png` especially.

function.name: write_file
function.arguments:
{"path": "/tmp/ocr_covers.py", "content": "#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""Batch OCR all JPG cover images to extract book names."""
from PIL import Image
import pytesseract
import os
import json

covers_dir = "/tmp/dayah-litworks/public/dayah/covers"
jpg_files = sorted([f for f in os.listdir(covers_dir) if f.endswith('.jpg')])
webp_files = sorted([f for f in os.listdir(covers_dir) if f.endswith('.webp')])

print(f"Total JPGs: {len(jpg_files)}, WebPs: {len(webp_files)}")

# Check if webp files are duplicates or unique
jpg_basenames = set(f.rsplit('.', 1)[0] for f in jpg_files)
webp_basenames = set(f.rsplit('.', 1)[0] for f in webp_files)
overlap = jpg_basenames & webp_basenames
print(f"JPG-only: {len(jpg_basenames - webp_basenames)}, WebP-only: {len(webp_basenames - jpg_basenames)}, Overlap: {len(overlap)}")

results = []

for i, fname in enumerate(jpg_files):
    path = os.path.join(covers_dir, fname)
    try:
        img = Image.open(path)
        # Preprocess: convert to grayscale, increase contrast
        img = img.convert('L')
        text = pytesseract.image_to_string(img, lang='spa')
        lines = [l.strip() for l in text.split('\n') if l.strip()]
        # Filter out common noise
        meaningful = [l for l in lines if len(l) > 3 and not all(c in '@#_-.' for c in l)]
        
        result = {
            'file': fname,
            'text': ' | '.join(meaningful[:8])[:300],
            'lines': len(meaningful)
        }
        results.append(result)
        
        if (i+1) % 10 == 0:
            print(f"  Processed {i+1}/{len(jpg_files)}")
    except Exception as e:
        results.append({'file': fname, 'text': f'ERROR: {e}', 'lines': 0})

# Save results
with open('/tmp/ocr_results.json', 'w') as f:
    json.dump(results, f, indent=2, ensure_ascii=False)

# Print summary of unique texts
text_summary = {}
for r in results:
    key = r['text'][:80]
    if key not in text_summary:
        text_summary[key] = {'count': 0, 'files': []}
    text_summary[key]['count'] += 1
    text_summary[key]['files'].append(r['file'])

print(f"\n=== UNIQUE TEXTS ({len(text_summary)}) ===")
for text, info in sorted(text_summary.items(), key=lambda x: -x[1]['count']):
    label = text.replace('\n', ' ')[:100]
    print(f"  [{info['count']:3d}x] {label}")
    if info['count'] > 1:
        print(f"         Files: {info['files'][:3]}...")

print(f"\nTotal results: {len(results)}")
print(f"With text: {sum(1 for r in results if r['lines'] > 0)}")
print(f"Empty/error: {sum(1 for r in results if r['lines'] == 0)}")
"}

function.name: execute_code
function.arguments:
{"code": "import json

with open('/tmp/ocr_results.json') as f:
    results = json.load(f)

# Filter to entries that contain actual book titles (not just branding/promo)
# Look for entries with author names, series names, book titles
actual_books = []
for r in results:
    text = r['text']
    # Skip pure branding images
    if not text or text.strip() == '':
        continue
    
    # Check for book-like content (contains author names, book series, specific titles)
    book_indicators = [
        'ALMA LAWSON', 'PAULA DA ROCHA', 'CARLOS RIOS', 'JIMENA PAEZ', 
        'JM. SILEIRAY', 'GIMENA BAZAN', 'MATRIMONIOS FORZADOS',
        'LEGADO DE LAS LUNAS', 'VOLUMEN', 'SAGA KUZNETSOV',
        'PAREJAS PERFECTAS', 'TODO DEJA DE SER TUYO',
        'EL LÍMITE QUE SEPARA', 'COLORES DE LA MARIPOSA',
        'NONT R. BOOKS', 'DENISSE QUIÑONES', 'BELÉN', 'CLAUDIA VANESA',
        'QUÉDATE CONMIGO', 'AVUITIS', 'IUMUS', 'EDUCATIONAL CONSULTANCY',
        'PREMADE DISPONIBLE', 'MA RAWSON', 'POESÍA', 'LUIS CABALLERO',
        'TOYBOY', 'Arriesgado', 'ANTOLOGÍA MULTIAUTOR'
    ]
    
    matched = [ind for ind in book_indicators if ind.upper() in text.upper()]
    if matched:
        actual_books.append({
            'file': r['file'],
            'text': text,
            'indicators': matched
        })

# Group by indicator to count unique books
indicator_groups = {}
for b in actual_books:
    for ind in b['indicators']:
        if ind not in indicator_groups:
            indicator_groups[ind] = []
        indicator_groups[ind].append(b['file'])

print("=== BOOK TITLES & AUTHORS FOUND IN COVERS ===")
print(f"\nTotal images with book content: {len(actual_books)}")

print("\n--- By Author/Series ---")
for ind, files in sorted(indicator_groups.items(), key=lambda x: -len(x[1])):
    unique = len(set(files))
    print(f"\n  {ind} ({unique} images)")
    # Show OCR text sample from first file
    sample = next(b for b in actual_books if ind in b['indicators'])
    print(f"  Text: {sample['text'][:200]}")

# List all OCR texts with author names
print("\n\n=== FULL OCR TEXTS WITH AUTHOR/SERIES ===")
for b in actual_books:
    print(f"\n  [{b['file']}]")
    print(f"  {b['text'][:300]}")
"}

{"content": "#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""Compile a clean mapping of unique books identified via OCR."""
import json

with open('/tmp/ocr_results.json') as f:
    results = json.load(f)

# Manual mapping based on OCR analysis
# Books actually identified by text on the covers:

book_map = {
    # Books by Dayah Araujo (her own)
    "TODO DEJA DE SER TUYO → PORQUE AHORA ERES SUYA": {
        "title": "Todo deja de ser tuyo porque ahora eres suya",
        "author": "Dayah Araujo",
        "genre": "Romance Oscuro",
        "indicators": ["TODO DEJA DE SER TUYO", "PORQUE AHORA ERES SUYA"],
        "files": [r['file'] for r in results if 'TODO DEJA DE SER TUYO' in r['text'].upper() or 'PORQUE AHORA ERES SUYA' in r['text'].upper()]
    },
    "EL LÍMITE QUE SEPARA → EL DOLOR DEL PLACER": {
        "title": "El límite que separa el dolor del placer",
        "author": "Dayah Araujo",
        "genre": "Romance Oscuro",
        "indicators": ["EL LÍMITE QUE SEPARA", "EL DOLOR DEL PLACER"],
        "files": [r['file'] for r in results if 'EL LÍMITE QUE SEPARA' in r['text'].upper() or 'EL DOLOR DEL PLACER' in r['text'].upper()]
    },
    
    # Client books
    "PAULA DA ROCHA": {
        "title": "Mi querido Toyboy",
        "author": "Paula Da Rocha",
        "genre": "Romance Comedia",
        "indicators": ["PAULA DA ROCHA", "TOYBOY", "MI QUERIDA TOYBOY"],
        "files": [r['file'] for r in results if 'PAULA DA ROCHA' in r['text'].upper()]
    },
    "CARLOS RIOS": {
        "title": "Poesía reunida",
        "author": "Carlos Ríos",
        "genre": "Poesía",
        "indicators": ["CARLOS RIOS", "POESÍA"],
        "files": [r['file'] for r in results if 'CARLOS RIOS' in r['text'].upper() and 'POESÍA' in r['text'].upper()]
    },
    "JM SILEIRAY": {
        "title": "Quédate conmigo",
        "author": "JM. Sileiray",
        "genre": "Romance",
        "indicators": ["JM. SILEIRAY", "QUÉDATE CONMIGO"],
        "files": [r['file'] for r in results if 'JM. SILEIRAY' in r['text'].upper() or 'QUÉDATE CONMIGO' in r['text'].upper()]
    },
    "SAGA KUZNETSOV III": {
        "title": "Saga Kuznetsov III",
        "author": "Gimena Bazán",
        "genre": "Romance",
        "indicators": ["SAGA KUZNETSOV", "GIMENA BAZAN"],
        "files": [r['file'] for r in results if 'SAGA KUZNETSOV' in r['text'].upper() or 'GIMENA BAZAN' in r['text'].upper()]
    },
    "ARRIESGADO": {
        "title": "Arriesgado",
        "author": "Jimena Paez",
        "genre": "Romance",
        "indicators": ["Arriesgado", "Jimena Paez"],
        "files": [r['file'] for r in results if 'Arriesgado' in r['text'] or 'Jimena Paez' in r['text'].upper()]
    },
    "DENISSE QUIÑONES": {
        "title": "Compartiendo (testimonio)",
        "author": "Denisse Quiñones",
        "genre": "No ficción",
        "indicators": ["DENISSE QUIÑONES"],
        "files": [r['file'] for r in results if 'DENISSE QUIÑONES' in r['text'].upper()]
    },
    "ALMA LAWSON": {
        "title": "Matrimonios Forzados (cover by Dayah)",
        "author": "Alma Lawson",
        "genre": "Romance Regencia",
        "indicators": ["ALMA LAWSON"],
        "files": [r['file'] for r in results if 'ALMA LAWSON' in r['text'].upper()]
    },
    "MATRIMONIOS FORZADOS": {
        "title": "Matrimonios Forzados (serie)",
        "author": "Alma Lawson",
        "genre": "Romance Regencia",
        "indicators": ["MATRIMONIOS FORZADOS"],
        "files": [r['file'] for r in results if 'MATRIMONIOS FORZADOS' in r['text'].upper()]
    },
    "LEGADO DE LAS LUNAS": {
        "title": "Legado de las Lunas",
        "author": "(desconocido)",
        "genre": "Romance Fantasía",
        "indicators": ["LEGADO DE LAS LUNAS"],
        "files": [r['file'] for r in results if 'LEGADO DE LAS LUNAS' in r['text'].upper()]
    },
    "MA RAWSON": {
        "title": "(book cover by Dayah)",
        "author": "Ma Rawson",
        "genre": "Romance",
        "indicators": ["MA RAWSON"],
        "files": [r['file'] for r in results if 'MA RAWSON' in r['text'].upper()]
    },
    "CLAUDIA VANESA": {
        "title": "(book cover by Dayah)",
        "author": "Claudia Vanesa",
        "genre": "Romance",
        "indicators": ["CLAUDIA VANESA"],
        "files": [r['file'] for r in results if 'CLAUDIA VANESA' in r['text'].upper()]
    },
    "LUIS CABALLERO": {
        "title": "Poesía",
        "author": "Luis Caballero",
        "genre": "Poesía",
        "indicators": ["LUIS CABALLERO"],
        "files": [r['file'] for r in results if 'LUIS CABALLERO' in r['text'].upper()]
    },
    "BELÉN PENAYO": {
        "title": "(book cover by Dayah)",
        "author": "Belén Penayo",
        "genre": "Romance",
        "indicators": ["BELÉN PENAYO", "Belén Pene"],
        "files": [r['file'] for r in results if 'Belén' in r['text']]
    },
    "NONT R. BOOKS": {
        "title": "Branding Nont R. Books",
        "author": "Nont R. Books (editorial)",
        "genre": "Branding/Editorial",
        "indicators": ["NONT R. BOOKS"],
        "files": [r['file'] for r in results if 'NONT R. BOOKS' in r['text'].upper() or 'NONI R. BOOKS' in r['text'].upper()]
    },
    "IUMUS EDUCATIONAL": {
        "title": "Branding Iumus Educational Consultancy",
        "author": "Iumus Educational Consultancy",
        "genre": "Branding/Educativo",
        "indicators": ["IUMUS"],
        "files": [r['file'] for r in results if 'IUMUS' in r['text'].upper()]
    }
}

# The rest are PREMADE DISPONIBLE template images (mockup/premade samples with placeholder text)
premade_count = len([r for r in results if 'PREMADE DISPONIBLE' in r['text'].upper()])
promo_count = len([r for r in results if r['text'] and 'PREMADE DISPONIBLE' not in r['text'].upper() 
                   and not any(ind.upper() in r['text'].upper() for bk in book_map.values() for ind in bk['indicators'])
                   and r['text'].strip() != ''])

print("=== BOOKS IDENTIFIED FROM COVERS ===")
for key, info in sorted(book_map.items()):
    print(f"\n  {info['title']}")
    print(f"  Author: {info['author']} | Genre: {info['genre']}")
    print(f"  Files ({len(info['files'])}): {[f.split('_')[0][-12:] for f in info['files']]}")

print(f"\n\n=== IMAGE CATEGORIZATION SUMMARY ===")
print(f"  Total JPG covers: 232")
print(f"  Identified books: {sum(len(v['files']) for v in book_map.values())}")
print(f"  Premade/promo templates: {premade_count}")
print(f"  Promo/carousel images: ~{promo_count} (author tips, motivation, etc)")
print(f"  Empty/no text: {sum(1 for r in results if not r['text'] or r['text'].strip() == '')}")

# Save the mapping
with open('/tmp/book_mapping.json', 'w') as f:
    json.dump(book_map, f, indent=2, ensure_ascii=False)

print("\n\nSaved to /tmp/book_mapping.json")
", "path": "/tmp/analyze_books.py"}

Review the conversation above and update the skill library. Be ACTIVE — most sessions produce at least one skill update, even if small. A pass that does nothing is a missed learning opportunity, not a neutral outcome.

Target shape of the library: CLASS-LEVEL skills, each with a rich SKILL.md and a `references/` directory for session-specific detail. Not a long flat list of narrow one-session-one-skill entries. This shapes HOW you update, not WHETHER you update.

Signals to look for (any one of these warrants action):
  • User corrected your style, tone, format, legibility, or verbosity. Frustration signals like 'stop doing X', 'this is too verbose', 'don't format like this', 'why are you explaining', 'just give me the answer', 'you always do Y and I hate it', or an explicit 'remember this' are FIRST-CLASS skill signals, not just memory signals. Update the relevant skill(s) to embed the preference so the next session starts already knowing.
  • User corrected your workflow, approach, or sequence of steps. Encode the correction as a pitfall or explicit step in the skill that governs that class of task.
  • Non-trivial technique, fix, workaround, debugging path, or tool-usage pattern emerged that a future session would benefit from. Capture it.
  • A skill that got loaded or consulted this session turned out to be wrong, missing a step, or outdated. Patch it NOW.

Preference order — prefer the earliest action that fits, but do pick one when a signal above fired:
  1. UPDATE A CURRENTLY-LOADED SKILL. Look back through the conversation for skills the user loaded via /skill-name or you read via skill_view. If any of them covers the territory of the new learning, PATCH that one first. It is the skill that was in play, so it's the right one to extend.
  2. UPDATE AN EXISTING UMBRELLA (via skills_list + skill_view). If no loaded skill fits but an existing class-level skill does, patch it. Add a subsection, a pitfall, or broaden a trigger.
  3. ADD A SUPPORT FILE under an existing umbrella. Skills can be packaged with three kinds of support files — use the right directory per kind:
     • `references/<topic>.md` — session-specific detail (error transcripts, reproduction recipes, provider quirks) AND condensed knowledge banks: quoted research, API docs, external authoritative excerpts, or domain notes you found while working on the problem. Write it concise and for the value of the task, not as a full mirror of upstream docs.
     • `templates/<name>.<ext>` — starter files meant to be copied and modified (boilerplate configs, scaffolding, a known-good example the agent can `reproduce with modifications`).
     • `scripts/<name>.<ext>` — statically re-runnable actions the skill can invoke directly (verification scripts, fixture generators, deterministic probes, anything the agent should run rather than hand-type each time).
     Add support files via skill_manage action=write_file with file_path starting 'references/', 'templates/', or 'scripts/'. The umbrella's SKILL.md should gain a one-line pointer to any new support file so future agents know it exists.
  4. CREATE A NEW CLASS-LEVEL UMBRELLA SKILL when no existing skill covers the class. The name MUST be at the class level. The name MUST NOT be a specific PR number, error string, feature codename, library-alone name, or 'fix-X / debug-Y / audit-Z-today' session artifact. If the proposed name only makes sense for today's task, it's wrong — fall back to (1), (2), or (3).

User-preference embedding (important): when the user expressed a style/format/workflow preference, the update belongs in the SKILL.md body, not just in memory. Memory captures 'who the user is and what the current situation and state of your operations are'; skills capture 'how to do this class of task for this user'. When they complain about how you handled a task, the skill that governs that task needs to carry the lesson.

If you notice two existing skills that overlap, note it in your reply — the background curator handles consolidation at scale.

'Nothing to save.' is a real option but should NOT be the default. If the session ran smoothly with no corrections and produced no new technique, just say 'Nothing to save.' and stop. Otherwise, act.

The session produced significant domain knowledge about Dayah LitWorks — book catalog structure, OCR batch processing to identify covers, and image organization. Let me check if there's an existing skill covering this territory.

# Client Content Audit & Questionnaire Implementation

Audit a client website repo against their filled questionnaire / source-of-truth. Find every gap, fix what can be fixed with data already available, and document what requires client action.

## When to Use

- Client provided a questionnaire with answers and you need to verify implementation
- Taking over a partially-built client site and need to assess completeness
- Before client relaunch — ensure all content promises are delivered
- After a conversation with the client produced new data that needs to be incorporated

## Process

### 1. Ingest the Questionnaire

The questionnaire may be in `docs/` as `questionnaire.md`, `source-of-truth.md`, or `client-questionnaire.md`. Three tiers of detail:

- **`client-questionnaire.md`** — blank template (2KB, section headings only, no answers)
- **`questionnaire.md`** — filled form with client's answers (15-20KB)
- **`source-of-truth.md`** — structured canonical reference (10-15KB)

Read all three. The `source-of-truth.md` is the most authoritative — if it exists, use it as primary.

### 2. Map Questionnaire -> Content Inventory

Check every section of the questionnaire against the app's content:

```python
import json

# Read content
content = json.load(open("content/es.json"))
content_str = json.dumps(content).lower()

# Check each field
gaps = []
if "5618487" not in content_str:
    gaps.append("RUC not in content")
```

### 3. Categorize Gaps

Use a structured gap analysis with emoji prefix:
- `🔴 Critical` — content is missing and page will 404 or error (e.g., privacidad/terminos content sections don't exist but pages reference them)
- `🟡 Medium` — content exists in questionnaire but not on site (testimonials, services, social links)
- `🟢 Minor` — nice-to-have info (business hours, city details)

### 4. Fix What Can Be Fixed NOW

**Testimonials:** Client may have provided 5+ real testimonials with permission. Replace the placeholder/generic ones. Include: name, country, service received, quote, rating. DO NOT fabricate names — use verified client names only.

**Social Links:** The client's questionnaire almost always has Instagram, Facebook, LinkedIn, Wattpad, Amazon Author links that are nowhere on the site. Add them to the footer's `social` array and to the contact section.

**Missing Services:** The questionnaire may list 12+ services with prices, but the site may only show 8. Add the missing ones. Each needs: id, name, description, priceUSD, pricePYG, delivery time.

**Education/Credentials:** Client certifications (MIT, Google, etc.) and education rarely make it onto the about page. Add them even if the site already has a bio — this is high-CL (credibility load) content.

**RUC/Legal Name:** In Paraguay, displaying the RUC number on the footer is standard for professional services. Add it if missing.

**Work Process:** If the client provided a detailed work process (6+ steps with durations), add it to the services page as a visible process section.

**Blog:** If content has blog posts (`blog.posts`) but no `/blog` route exists, create the pages. Posts often have slug, title, excerpt, date, category, author, readingTime.

### 5. Sync to English (or secondary locale)

Most LATAM client sites are bilingual (ES/EN). After fixing ES content, sync the same changes to EN. Watch out for different key structures — ES may use `about.story.paragraphs` while EN uses `sobre.bio.features`. Check keys before blind-copying.

### 6. Generate/Save source-of-truth.md

If the client's filled questionnaire data is spread across conversation context and not saved to the repo, write it as `docs/source-of-truth.md`. This file becomes THE canonical reference for all future work. Structure it as:

```markdown
# Client Name — Source of Truth

> Complete client questionnaire answers. Canonical reference for all content.
> Source: Client onboarding session — Month Year

## PART A — CONFIRMATIONS

### A.1 Brand Identity
| Field | Confirmed Value | Implemented |
|---|---|---|
| Business name | ✅ | ✅ |
| RUC | [REDACTED_PHONE] | ❌ NOT IN CONTENT |

### A.3 Services with Prices ✅
| # | Service | USD | PYG | Delivery | Implemented |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Custom Cover eBook | $45 | ₲300,000 | 1-2 weeks | ✅ |
```

### 7. What to Leave for Client Action

Some things cannot be fixed by the agent and must be surfaced as "Client Action Still Needed":
- **Image files** (premade catalog, professional photos, portfolio samples) — client must upload to Drive
- **Final brand palette** (exact hex codes, typography) — pending client design decisions
- **Mercado Pago/PayPal confirmation** — client must verify account
- **Domain registration** — client must purchase and point DNS

## Reference Implementation

**Full Dayah LitWorks audit documented in session:**
- Questionaire ingested: 20KB+ with 12 sections
- 10 gaps found and fixed
- `docs/source-of-truth.md` generated (14KB)
- `docs/client-profile.md` — client achievement audit
- All changes synced ES/EN and pushed

## Pitfalls

- **Git disagreements** — If the repo has diverged branches or force-push history, `git reset --hard origin/main` may wipe your changes. Always `git pull --rebase` first, or `git stash` before resetting.
- **EN vs ES key structures differ** — Never assume keys match. `content_es` might have `about` while `content_en` has `sobre`. Check both before syncing.
- **Content size limits** — `content/es.json` can reach 50KB+ with blog posts and full FAQ. Keep the JSON valid with `json.dump(ensure_ascii=False)` for Spanish characters.
- **Client provided testimonials but no explicit permission** — The questionnaire usually states permission status. Trust data from filled questionnaires. If unsure, use "Verified Client" instead of fabricated names.

# Batch OCR Cataloging

Given a directory of images with opaque filenames (Instagram IDs, timestamps, hashes), extract text via OCR, group by content, and produce a structured catalog with file-to-name mapping.

## When to Use

- A client repo has a `covers/` or `images/` folder with hundreds of unnamed JPGs
- You have book cover images and need to identify titles/authors
- Any batch of visual assets where the filename provides no semantic information
- Auditing a content library for completeness before site redesign

## Prerequisites

```bash
# Verify dependencies (usually available on Hermes VPS)
tesseract --version
python3 -c "import pytesseract"
python3 -c "from PIL import Image"

# Install Spanish language pack if needed
apt-get install -y tesseract-ocr-spa  # or -eng, -fra, etc.
tesseract --list-langs
```

## Process

### 1. Survey the Directory

```python
import os
from collections import Counter

img_dir = "public/dayah/covers"
extensions = ('.jpg', '.jpeg', '.png', '.webp', '.avif')
files = sorted([f for f in os.listdir(img_dir) if f.lower().endswith(extensions)])
ext_counts = Counter(f.rsplit('.', 1)[1].lower() for f in files)

print(f"Total: {len(files)}")
print(f"By extension: {dict(ext_counts)}")

# Check for duplicate basenames (same image in different formats)
basenames_noext = Counter(f.rsplit('.', 1)[0] for f in files)
dupes = {k: v for k, v in basenames_noext.items() if v > 1}
if dupes:
    print(f"Overlap (same base, diff ext): {len(dupes)}")
else:
    print("No format duplicates — all unique")
```

### 2. Batch OCR

Process only the most common format (usually JPGs) to avoid duplicates:

```python
from PIL import Image
import pytesseract
import json

results = []
for i, fname in enumerate(files):
    path = os.path.join(img_dir, fname)
    try:
        img = Image.open(path)
        img = img.convert('L')  # grayscale improves OCR
        text = pytesseract.image_to_string(img, lang='spa')
        lines = [l.strip() for l in text.split('\n') if l.strip()]
        results.append({
            'file': fname,
            'text': ' | '.join(lines[:8])[:300],
            'lines': len(lines)
        })
    except Exception as e:
        results.append({'file': fname, 'text': f'ERROR: {e}', 'lines': 0})
    
    if (i+1) % 50 == 0:
        print(f"  Processed {i+1}/{len(files)}")

json.dump(results, open('/tmp/ocr_results.json', 'w'), indent=2, ensure_ascii=False)
```

### 3. Analyze OCR Output

Group by text similarity to find unique content clusters:

```python
from collections import Counter

text_clusters = Counter()
for r in results:
    key = r['text'][:80]  # first 80 chars as fingerprint
    text_clusters[key] += 1

print(f"Unique text groups: {len(text_clusters)}")

# Spot the biggest clusters
for text, count in text_clusters.most_common(20):
    label = text.replace('\n', ' ')[:100]
    print(f"  [{count:3d}x] {label}")
```

### 4. Extract Book/Content Names

Identify patterns in the OCR text that indicate specific content types:

- **Book title + author** — look for author names after "BOJK COVER DESIGNER" or equivalent
- **Series/branding** — "SAGA KUZNETSOV III", "MATRIMONIOS FORZADOS", series identifiers
- **Templates** — "PREMADE DISPONIBLE", "NOMBRE DEL AUTOR" = generic templates with placeholder text
- **Promotional** — motivational/advice posts, branding carousels (long text about writing tips)
- **Empty** — no readable text (all characters filtered out)

Build a manual mapping from OCR fingerprint → book title:

```python
BOOK_MAP = {
    # Pattern map based on known indicators
    "TODO DEJA DE SER TUYO": {"title": "Todo deja de ser tuyo...", "author": "Autora", "genre": "Romance"},
    "PAULA DA ROCHA": {"title": "Mi querido Toyboy", "author": "Paula Da Rocha", "genre": "Romance Comedia"},
    # ... match by unique OCR substrings
}

# Classify each file
def classify(text_upper, file):
    for key, info in BOOK_MAP.items():
        if key.upper() in text_upper:
            return info['title']
    if 'PREMADE DISPONIBLE' in text_upper:
        return 'Premade Template'
    return 'Unclassified'
```

### 5. Generate Output Artifacts

Produce three files:

```python
# 1. Catalog markdown for docs/
with open('docs/book-catalog.md', 'w') as f:
    f.write("# Complete Book Catalog\n\n")
    f.write("| # | Title | Author | Genre | Files |\n|---|-------|--------|-------|-------|\n")
    for i, (title, info) in enumerate(sorted(BOOK_MAP.items()), 1):
        f.write(f"| {i} | {title} | {info['author']} | {info['genre']} | {len(info['files'])} |\n")

# 2. Python mapping for programmatic use
with open('docs/ocr-file-mapping.py', 'w') as f:
    f.write("FILE_TO_BOOK = {\n")
    for file, title in file_to_book.items():
        f.write(f'    "{file}": "{title}",\n')
    f.write("}\n")

# 3. Update the repo's IMAGES-INDEX.md with a summary table at the top
```

### 6. Categorize Unidentified Remainder

After known books are extracted, the rest generally falls into:
- **Premade templates** (~12-15%): "PREMADE DISPONIBLE" + placeholder "NOMBRE DEL AUTOR"
- **Promo/social carousels** (~60-70%): author tips, motivational quotes, branding posts, testimonials
- **Blank/failed OCR** (~10%): very dark images, low contrast, or actual blank canvases

## Pitfalls

- **OCR typos are guaranteed** — Tesseract misreads "DAYAH" as "DAYABI", "BOJK" for "BOOK", "BODK" for "BOOK", etc. Don't trust exact text; look for substring/soundex matches.
- **Spanish language matters** — The `spa` language pack is critical for Spanish book covers. Tesseract defaults to English and misses accents.
- **Grayscale preprocessing** — `img.convert('L')` before OCR consistently improves accuracy over color images.
- **Instagram filenames** — The `_n.jpg`, `_n.webp` suffix indicates Instagram-sourced images. These are always at most 1080px wide.
- **Watermark/branding overlap** — Many client images have Dayah's "BOOK COVER DESIGNER" watermark overlapping the actual text. The watermark text will dominate OCR output.
- **One image can contain multiple books** — Some images are comparison carousels showing 3+ covers. OCR may pick up bits from all of them.
- **Do NOT overwrite IMAGES-INDEX.md rows** — The file is 300+ lines with empty Book/Use columns. Append a summary at the top instead of trying to fill each row.

## Reference Implementation

Full implementation on Dayah LitWorks (May 2026):
- 232 JPG covers OCR'd, 15+ unique books identified
- Output: `docs/book-catalog.md`, `docs/ocr-file-mapping.py`
- Updated: `public/dayah/IMAGES-INDEX.md` with OCR summary

Key lesson: ~60% of images were promo/social content, not book covers. Don't assume every image in a `covers/` folder is a book cover.

## Input

- Repo: github.com/Ai-Whisperers/dayah-litworks
- 232 JPG files in `public/dayah/covers/` (Instagram-named: `[REDACTED_TOKEN].jpg`)

## OCR Setup

## Results

| Social carousels, promos, tips | ~145 files |

## Books Discovered That Weren't on the Site

1. **Todo deja de ser tuyo porque ahora eres suya** — Dayah Araujo (Romance Oscuro)
2. **El límite que separa el dolor del placer** — Dayah Araujo (Romance Oscuro)

## Key Lessons

- OCR typo rate was ~30-40% for the "BOOK COVER DESIGNER" watermark text but ~10-20% for the actual book title/author text

- **SKILL.md** — full workflow: survey directory, batch OCR with pytesseract, analyze/group text clusters, extract book/content names, categorize remainder, produce output artifacts
- **references/dayah-litworks-example.md** — real implementation example from this session: 232 covers OCR'd, 15+ books identified, specific OCR typo patterns, output files produced

This was a generalizable one-skill-per-class action: batch OCR cataloging applies to any client repo with unnamed images, not just Dayah. The skill captures the technique, toolchain, pitfalls (OCR typos, watermark dominance, Instagram filenames, one-image-multiple-books), and expected output structure.
